inputs.label: Retirement Age
inputs.hint: Preservation age is 60; Age Pension eligibility is 67

inputs.label: Current Super Balance (A$)
inputs.hint: Check your fund's online portal or latest statement

inputs.label: Extra Contributions / Year (A$)
inputs.hint: Salary sacrifice or personal contributions on top of SG

inputs.label: Expected Investment Return (%)
inputs.hint: Balanced option long-run average ~7% before fund fees

howToSteps.name: Enter Your Age and Current Super Balance
howToSteps.text: Enter your current age and super balance. Check your MySuper dashboard or latest annual statement. Even small balances grow significantly over decades due to compounding.

howToSteps.name: Set Salary and Super Rate
howToSteps.text: Enter your gross salary and the applicable super guarantee rate. From 1 July 2025, the SG rate increased to 12% for all employees. If your contract says 'super on top', your employer pays this amount in addition to your salary.

howToSteps.name: Add Extra Contributions (Optional)
howToSteps.text: If you are salary sacrificing or making personal contributions above the SG, enter the additional annual amount. Extra contributions inside the $30,000 concessional cap are taxed at only 15% in the fund.

howToSteps.name: Review Projected Balance
howToSteps.text: The calculator shows your projected super balance at retirement, total contributions made, total investment growth, and an estimated annual sustainable drawdown using the 4% rule.

faq.q: What is the Superannuation Guarantee rate in Australia for FY2026?
faq.a: The Superannuation Guarantee (SG) rate increased to 12% from 1 July 2025 (FY2026). It was 11.5% in FY2025 and 11% in FY2024. The rate will remain at 12% indefinitely under current legislation. Employers must pay this amount on top of (or as part of, depending on contract) your wages into your nominated super fund.

faq.q: How much super should I have at my age?
faq.a: The Association of Superannuation Funds of Australia (ASFA) provides benchmark super balances by age. Approximate benchmarks: age 30: $50,000–$60,000; age 40: $130,000–$150,000; age 50: $260,000–$300,000; age 60: $450,000–$520,000. These are median benchmarks — many Australians have less. ASFA estimates a comfortable retirement requires $595,000 for singles and $690,000 for couples (2025 figures).

faq.q: What is the concessional contributions cap?
faq.a: The concessional contributions cap is $30,000 per financial year (FY2025–26). Concessional contributions include employer SG contributions plus any salary sacrifice contributions and personal contributions you claim a tax deduction for. Contributions within this cap are taxed at 15% in the super fund. Contributions exceeding the cap are included in your assessable income at your marginal tax rate, with a 15% tax offset.

faq.q: When can I access my superannuation?
faq.a: The preservation age in Australia is 60 for anyone born after 30 June 1964. You can access your super on or after preservation age once you have retired (met a condition of release). You can also access super from age 60 while still working via a Transition to Retirement (TTR) pension. Compulsory access under the Retirement Income Covenant begins at age 65 regardless of work status. Age Pension eligibility commences at 67.

faq.q: Should I contribute more to super or pay off my mortgage first?
faq.a: This is a common dilemma. Key factors: your marginal tax rate (higher earners benefit more from super's 15% contributions tax), your mortgage interest rate vs expected super return, and your time horizon. A common approach is to split: make minimum SG, pay down mortgage aggressively, then increase super contributions as the mortgage decreases. Use the Super Balance and Mortgage Calculator together to model both scenarios.

examples.heading: Age 35, $85,000 salary, $85,000 current super
examples.text: Starting with $85,000 in super at age 35, earning $95,000/year with 12% SG contributions ($11,400/year) and 7% annual investment return. By retirement at 67 (32 years), the projected super balance is approximately $1.28 million. Total contributions: approximately $486,000 (employer SG); investment growth: approximately $709,000.

examples.heading: Age 45, $150,000 salary, salary sacrifice $15,000/year
examples.text: A 45-year-old with $250,000 in super, earning $150,000/year. SG at 12% = $18,000/year. Additional salary sacrifice of $15,000/year (total concessional $33,000 — slightly above $30,000 cap; model uses $30,000 cap). Projected balance at 67: approximately $1.6 million. The salary sacrifice on top of SG reduces taxable income and accelerates super growth significantly.
